errors: ; The value set “Reason for not performing Chlamydia Screening” has an id of “TBD”, which is a placeholder rather than a real, clinically defined OID.; The age filter uses “Less” 24 (exclusive), thereby excluding patients who are exactly 24 years old, whereas most screening guidelines include patients up to 24 years inclusive.

The ProcedureLookBack function uses a look‑back interval of 1 month (Quantity 1 month) to determine if a colonoscopy has been performed. Clinically, colon cancer screening with colonoscopy is recommended every 10 years for adults aged 50 and older, not every month. This mismatch makes the recommendation logic clinically unreasonable.

The ProcedureLookBack function uses a 1‑month lookback interval to determine whether a PSA test, DRE, or biopsy has been performed. Clinical guidelines for prostate cancer screening recommend a much longer interval (typically 1–2 years) between screening tests, not a 1‑month window. This short lookback period would trigger unnecessary screening recommendations and does not align with standard practice.

The logic incorrectly excludes patients who already have an osteopenia diagnosis or an osteoporotic fracture from needing screening, which contradicts USPSTF recommendations that women 65 years or older should be screened regardless of these conditions. Additionally, the “Has Osteoporotic Fracture” check uses a non‑null clinicalStatus rather than a specific fracture code, which may miss relevant fracture records.

["Minimum Age set to 25 excludes adults 18‑24 who should also be screened for hypertension; LookBack interval of 6 months is shorter than recommended screening intervals (3‑5 years for adults 18‑39, annually for 40+)."]
[HbA1c threshold 9 g% is not clinically reasonable; typical threshold for diabetes diagnosis is 6.5 % (or 48 mmol/mol); the unit “g%” is not a standard HbA1c measurement.]

The ELM implementation does not enforce the USPSTF requirement of a 20‑pack‑year smoking history, nor does it include former smokers who quit within the past 15 years. The “Has Smoking History” expression only checks for an active “Tobacco User” condition, which is insufficient to meet the guideline criteria.

The guideline values in the ELM do not align with current colorectal cancer screening recommendations. Specifically, the minimum age is set to 60 years and the maximum to 75 years, whereas most guidelines (e.g., USPSTF 2021) recommend initiating average‑risk screening at age 45 (or 50 in earlier guidelines) and continuing until age 75. Additionally, the “Has Recent Colorectal Screening” condition requires a screening observation within the last 2 years, which is not consistent with modality‑specific intervals (e.g., colonoscopy every 10 years, FIT annually, stool DNA every 3 years). These discrepancies make the values clinically unreasonable.

[Age threshold of 30 years is higher than CDC recommended 15 years; screening interval of 1 year may not align with guidelines for low‑risk adults; value set ID may not match standard HIV test codes; recommendation text lacks specificity; rationale is missing]

The BMI threshold for obesity is set at 35 kg/m², which is higher than the typical obesity cutoff of 30 kg/m² used in most prediabetes screening guidelines. This may exclude patients who are obese (BMI ≥ 30) but not class II obese (BMI ≥ 35) from recommended screening.

; The age range in the ELM (30–65 years) excludes women aged 21–29, whereas USPSTF recommends cervical cancer screening for all women aged 21–65.; The recommendation and rationale use cervical cytology (Pap smear) alone every 3 years for women 30–65, but the current USPSTF guideline recommends either Pap + HPV co‑testing every 5 years or Pap alone every 3 years for this age group. The ELM does not reflect the preferred co‑testing option.

The age threshold used in the “In Demographic” expression is 75 years, whereas the USPSTF guideline recommends falls risk screening for community‑dwelling adults **65 years or older**. This mismatch makes the clinical logic not fully aligned with the guideline.
The age threshold in the “In Demographic” expression is set to ≥ 25 years, which conflicts with the USPSTF recommendation that screening be performed for adults 18 years or older.

The logic excludes patients who have an osteopenia diagnosis or an osteoporotic fracture from the screening population. According to USPSTF guidelines, women 65 years or older should be screened for osteoporosis regardless of osteopenia status, and patients with a prior osteoporotic fracture should also receive a DXA scan. Therefore, the “Not Has Osteopenia Diagnosis” and “Not Has Osteoporotic Fracture” conditions are clinically inappropriate.
[Age range mismatch: logic uses 55-75 but USPSTF recommends 65-75 for AAA screening in men who have ever smoked.]

; The eligibility criteria require only an active tobacco user, whereas USPSTF guidelines specify a 20 pack‑year smoking history.; The age range is limited to ≥50 years, but the upper bound of 80 years is not enforced.; The recommendation states “annual LDCT,” yet the criteria only check for the absence of a recent LDCT within the last year, not that an annual screening is due.; The rationale references a 20 pack‑year history, which is not reflected in the logic.
The “Needs AAA Screening” expression does not include a check for male gender, whereas the USPSTF guideline specifies screening only for men aged 65‑75 who have ever smoked.

The logic for determining whether a colonoscopy has been performed is incorrect. The `ProcedureLookBack` function uses a look‑back interval of 1 month, and the `Colonoscopy` expression returns true if at least one colonoscopy was performed in the last month. This is the opposite of the intended screening rule, which should flag patients who have **not** had a colonoscopy in the last 10 years. Consequently, the `Recommendation` will incorrectly advise a colonoscopy for patients who have just had one, and will fail to recommend screening for those who are due. The screening interval and the inclusion criteria logic need to be corrected to reflect the standard guideline of a 10‑year interval.
